game_name: Joe & Mac 2: Lost in the Tropics

danger_signals.meaning: Direct combat threat; primary antagonists throughout levels

danger_signals.visual_form: Large sprite-scaled creatures (Stegosaurus, Pteranodon, Triceratops, Elasmosaurus, Tyrannosaurus)
danger_signals.meaning: Major environmental/encounter hazards; progression gates requiring defeat

danger_signals.visual_form: Boss enemy that transforms into warthog demon after initial defeat
danger_signals.meaning: Escalating threat; visual escalation signals second-phase danger

danger_signals.visual_form: Contextual level design (swamps, volcanoes, snowy mountains)
danger_signals.meaning: Thematic danger zones tied to setting; implied hazards from biome

safety_signals.visual_form: Pteranodon, Styracosaurus, Plesiosaurus rendered as rideable allies
safety_signals.meaning: Safe transit; cooperative entity that provides mechanical advantage

safety_signals.visual_form: Stone Age village hub with relationship mechanics
safety_signals.meaning: Safe zone for social/progression activities (courtship, marriage, child-rearing)

safety_signals.visual_form: Colorful stone rewards from defeated bosses
safety_signals.meaning: Achievement markers; visual proof of safe progression completion
Economic safety; repeatable resource accumulation signal

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Neanderthal enemies contrast with player character sophistication
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Stone Age village is player faction homebase

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Generic hostile prehistoric humanoids throughout levels
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Present across all six level types (valley, jungle, snow, swamp, volcano, final)

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Specific dinosaur species function as both obstacles (bosses) and allies (rideable)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Thematic creatures in each biome; swamp = Elasmosaurus, volcano = implied fire-dwelling species

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Valley and jungle levels with accessible dinosaur encounters
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies and obstacles scale with player skill progression

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Environmental variety increases (snow mountain, swamp biomes introduce visual complexity)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Boss encounters become more varied; friendly dinosaur mounts available for transit

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Volcano setting; Gork transforms to warthog demon form after first defeat
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: All previous bosses must be re-fought; visual escalation signals ultimate threat

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Village setting changes reflect player social choices (courtship → marriage → fatherhood)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Flower and meat gift-giving triggers relationship state changes

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Stone wheel accumulation displayed in UI/inventory
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Level replay enables resource grinding; currency allows progression gate passage

environmental_storytelling.example: Six distinct level environments (valley, jungle, snowy mountain, swamp, volcano) communicate setting progression and thematic escalation
Prehistoric megafauna throughout all levels anchors world as Stone Age with coexisting dinosaurs
Stone Age village serves as home base where social/relationship mechanics occur, establishing player faction identity
Specific dinosaur species (Stegosaurus, Pteranodon, Triceratops, Elasmosaurus, Tyrannosaurus, Gork) as level gates communicate progression stakes
Stone wheels as literal resource objects establish primitive economy and repeatable progression loop

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Present across all levels; generic threat; no faction-specific visual differentiation noted
Single appearance per level as gate boss; second appearance in final level suggests respawn mechanic
Grant player mobility advantage; visual distinction from hostile dinosaurs likely color/animation cues
Dual-phase encounter; visual transformation to warthog demon signals escalation after first defeat
Two-player synchronizable; relationship mechanics tied to village hub; marriage/fatherhood state visible

notes: Joe & Mac 2 uses a Stone Age/prehistoric setting with dinosaur integration as thematic worldbuilding rather than lore-grounded. Visual communication relies on sprite-scale hierarchy (boss dinosaurs larger), biome environmental theming, and hub-based social progression. Faction identity is minimal (protagonist caveman ninjas vs. generic Neanderthals vs. dinosaur ecosystem); world-state is primarily conveyed through level progression order and relationship/economic resource accumulation. Limited faction-specific visual markers; danger/safety distinctions rely on entity type (dinosaur species, enemy humanoids, rideable allies) rather than color-coded faction systems.
